title: Energy-space quantum walks: Thermalization without state convergence
authors: Alana Spak dos Santos, Renato Moreira Angelo
abstract: We introduce energy-space quantum walks as a minimal framework to investigate equilibration, thermalization, and irreversibility from an effective-dynamics perspective. By mapping the configuration space of a walk onto a ladder of energy eigenlevels, we reinterpret thermalization as transport in energy space, independently of microscopic system--bath details. At the classical level, the resulting birth--death--lazy dynamics leads to equilibration of the energy distribution and, under suitable conditions, to a Gibbs stationary state. We then embed this dynamics into a unitary, collision-assisted model in which coherence is controlled by a single parameter. A central result is a structural decoupling between population dynamics and coherence generation: while the populations evolve according to the classical process and relax to the Gibbs distribution, the full quantum state exhibits a persistent coherence-induced deviation from the thermal manifold. This establishes a minimal scenario of thermalization without state convergence, where equilibration occurs at the level of populations but not at the level of the full density operator. We quantify this effect using the thermal distance to the Gibbs state and derive perturbative bounds that relate the long-time deviation to classical transport properties. Our results show that coherence acts as a controllable and quantitatively bounded source of nonthermal behavior, providing a clear separation between classical equilibration and genuinely quantum corrections.
summary: This paper introduces energy-space quantum walks to study thermalization, showing that energy populations can equilibrate to a Gibbs state even when the full quantum state fails to converge to that state. By quantifying the deviation using the thermal distance, the authors demonstrate that coherence acts as a persistent, controllable source of non-thermal behavior, separating classical equilibration from quantum corrections.
main_problem: The paper investigates the fundamental difference between energy equilibration (population relaxation) and full quantum state thermalization in quantum walks.
main_result: It establishes a minimal scenario of 'thermalization without state convergence,' where energy populations reach a classical Gibbs distribution, but the full quantum state retains a persistent, coherence-induced deviation from the thermal manifold.
method: The analysis uses an effective-dynamics approach, mapping the system onto an energy ladder and comparing the time evolution of the full density operator against its diagonal (population) components.
model_or_system: The model is an energy-space quantum walk, where the configuration space is mapped onto a ladder of energy eigenlevels. The dynamics are governed by a unitary, collision-assisted quantum channel $\Phi_\mu$ parameterized by a coherence strength $\mu$.
key_observables: Thermal distance ($d_{th}(t)$) and the difference between the full thermal distance and the dephased distance, which quantifies the coherence contribution.
important_parameters: The coherence parameter $\mu$, which controls the quantum corrections, and the bias $b = p_-/p_+$ in the classical limit.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the underlying classical dynamics is ergodic and that the system can be effectively described by a minimal collision-assisted quantum walk structure.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the time evolution of the thermal distance, showing saturation at a finite, non-zero value when coherence ($\mu>0$) is present, contrasting this with the zero saturation seen in purely thermalizing cases.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the classical limit (birth-death-lazy dynamics) leading to Gibbs equilibrium. It then embeds this into a unitary, coherent model, systematically decomposing the evolution into classical (population) and quantum (coherence) parts to quantify the separation.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous, quantitative framework to distinguish between classical relaxation processes and genuine quantum non-equilibrium effects in open quantum systems, which is crucial for understanding thermalization in realistic quantum simulators.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Equilibration and thermalization in the incoherent energy- space dynamics for a system with equally spaced energies En = nE. The simulations use a truncated ladder n = 0, 1, . . . , N with N = 50, fixed lazy probability p0 = 0.1, and a normalized discrete Gaussian initial distribution (“cold state”) ℘0(n) ∝e−(n−n0)2/(2σ2)

FIG. 2. Thermal distance dth(t) (solid red line) and its dephased counterpart ddiag th (t) (dashed blue line), where ρβt is the Gibbs state with the same instantaneous mean energy as ρt. While the de- phased dynamics relaxes to the Gibbs state, dth(t) saturates at a finite value, revealing a persistent coherence-induced deviation from ther- mal equilibrium. Parameters as described in the text.

FIG. 4. Asymptotic thermal distance d∞(µ) as a function of the coherence parameter µ. Blue dots denote numerical results, dashed black lines indicate analytical bounds, and the red solid line corre- sponds to the first-order perturbative prediction. The linear scaling at small µ confirms the perturbative analysis, while deviations at larger µ signal the onset of nonperturbative effects. Parameters as described in the text.

FIG. 3. Time evolution of the thermal distance dth(t) for different values of the coherence parameter µ. For µ = 0, the dynamics re- duces to the classical case and dth(t) →0, while for µ > 0 the distance saturates at a finite value that increases with µ, indicating a growing coherence-induced deviation from the Gibbs state. Parame- ters as described in the text.
